title: The Last Pour
brief: Chief Adewale Bankole, 58, founder of Bankole & Sons Distillery, was found dead in his locked study at the family compound in Ikoyi the morning after his retirement dinner. A glass of his signature aged brandy sat half-finished on the desk. The coroner suspects poisoning. Four people had access to the study that night. Work the case and name who is responsible.

suspects.name: Tunde Bankole
suspects.motive: The new will, signed that week, cut his stake from 60% to 20%. He found out at dinner.
suspects.alibi: Says he left at 11:40pm and drove straight home. Gate log shows his car leaving at 11:43pm.
suspects.note: Calm, almost rehearsed. Asked twice when the will would be read.

suspects.name: Mrs. Folake Bankole
suspects.motive: A ₦400m insurance policy names her sole beneficiary. The marriage was cold.
suspects.alibi: Retired to the master bedroom at 11:15pm. The housekeeper confirms bringing her tea at 11:30pm.
suspects.note: Composed. Grief reads as relief to two witnesses.

suspects.name: Emeka Obi
suspects.motive: Owed the Chief ₦120m on a loan called in last month. A buy-out clause erases the debt on the Chief’s death.
suspects.alibi: Claims he was on a call with a Dubai supplier until past midnight. Has not produced the number.
suspects.note: Sweated through the interview. Volunteered an alibi before being asked.

suspects.name: Grace Aniekwe
suspects.motive: Quietly dismissed two weeks ago after a missing-cash accusation she denies. Still on the compound.
suspects.alibi: Served drinks all evening; last seen carrying the brandy decanter to the study at 11:20pm.
suspects.note: Nervous but cooperative. The only person who touched the decanter.

reports.title: Autopsy Report — A. Bankole
reports.subtitle: Lagos State Forensic Pathology Unit · Ref AP-2231

findings.heading: Toxicology
findings.detail: Lethal concentration of oleandrin in blood and gastric contents. Oleander is not pharmaceutical — it is brewed from crushed leaves of the ornamental shrub. Onset 30–60 minutes after ingestion. Consistent with the brandy as the vehicle.

findings.heading: Stomach contents
findings.detail: Brandy, trace canapé. No tablets, capsules, or pharmaceutical residue.

findings.heading: Note
findings.detail: A faint bruise on the right forearm, 2–3 days old, unrelated to time of death. Likely incidental.

reports.title: Scene Report — The Study
reports.subtitle: Crime Scene Unit · 14 Glover Road, Ikoyi

header.label: Door
header.value: Locked from inside · key on desk

findings.heading: Item 1 — Brandy glass
findings.detail: Oleandrin detected in residue. ONE set of prints: the victim’s. The glass was poured and handed to him, or he poured it himself from the decanter.

findings.heading: Item 2 — Brandy decanter
findings.detail: NO oleandrin in the decanter. The poison was in the glass only — not the bottle. Prints: victim, and a partial smudged set, gloved.

findings.heading: Item 3 — Pruned oleander shrub
findings.detail: Garden behind the kitchen. Fresh cuttings, leaves stripped. Secateurs found wiped clean and returned to the garden shed.

findings.heading: Item 4 — Torn betting slip
findings.detail: In the waste bin. Lagos racing, dated three weeks ago. Belongs to the gardener, off-duty that night.

findings.heading: Item 5 — Window
findings.detail: Latched from inside. No tampering. Drop to the courtyard is 4 metres.

findings.heading: Item 6 — Wall safe
findings.detail: Closed, undisturbed. ₦2.1m and the new will inside, intact.

findings.heading: Emeka Obi
findings.detail: Loan of ₦120m called in 31 days ago. Buy-out clause in the partnership deed voids the debt on a partner’s death. Strong financial motive — but a motive is not a method.

findings.heading: Mrs. Folake Bankole
findings.detail: ₦400m life policy, named beneficiary, premiums current. No unusual recent account activity.

witnesses.name: Dr. Ifeoma Nwosu
witnesses.statement: "Adewale toasted with the brandy at the table around 11pm and seemed perfectly well. He took a second glass into the study. He never drank with anyone watching after that."

witnesses.name: Sergeant Bello
witnesses.statement: "Tunde’s car left at 11:43. But the pedestrian side-gate by the generator house isn’t on the log — anyone on the compound could come and go on foot and I’d never see it."

witnesses.name: Aunty Comfort
witnesses.statement: "I heard shouting near midnight. A man’s voice. Could have been the TV. I went back to sleep."

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You carried the decanter to the study at 11:20. Did you pour the Chief’s glass?

lines.speaker: Grace
lines.text: I set the decanter down and left. He liked to pour his own. I swear I never touched his glass.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You were dismissed two weeks ago. Over money.

lines.speaker: Grace
lines.text: I did not take that money. Madam knows it. But yes — I was angry. Angry is not the same as this.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Do you know what oleander is?

lines.speaker: Grace
lines.text: The flower by the kitchen? It is poison, everyone in the house knows not to burn it. Why are you asking me this?

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You say you were on a call with Dubai until after midnight. Whose number?

lines.speaker: Emeka
lines.text: A supplier. I... I will have to find the contact. It was on WhatsApp, the call may not show.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: The loan he called in. ₦120m. That clears on his death.

lines.speaker: Emeka
lines.text: You think I would kill my partner of thirty years over money? I built that company with him.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: Were you in the study at any point that night?

lines.speaker: Emeka
lines.text: To say goodnight. Briefly. He was alive. He was pouring himself a drink. That is the truth.

timeline.event: Chief toasts with brandy at the table — visibly well.
Mrs. Bankole retires to the master bedroom.
Grace carries the brandy decanter to the study, then leaves.
Housekeeper brings Mrs. Bankole tea — confirms she is in her room.
Tunde’s car leaves through the main gate.
Emeka says goodnight in the study; Chief is alive, pouring a drink.
Neighbour hears a man shouting near midnight.

tools.name: Identity Lookup
tools.tagline: Pull a person’s record from the national registry.

results.note: A nursery permit. He grows ornamentals — oleander among them.
No criminal record, no flags. The cash accusation was never filed.

tools.name: Phone Records
tools.tagline: Subscriber and SIM registration for a number.

results.note: No Dubai call exists. His alibi has no record behind it.

tools.name: Call Log
tools.tagline: Inbound/outbound calls for a number on the night.

results.note: Two calls to his lawyer about the will — AFTER he left. Awake and anxious, not at the scene.

tools.name: Cell Triangulation
tools.tagline: Approximate a handset’s location from tower pings.

rows.value: Ikoyi · Glover Rd cell — still on-site
Ikoyi · Glover Rd cell — left after est. time of death

results.note: He never left. On the compound through the time of death — long after his "goodnight."
Tunde’s handset is in Lekki from 11:50. He really did go home.

tools.name: Crime Database
tools.tagline: Prior records, known associates, open flags.

rows.label: Result
rows.value: No priors on any named party

results.note: Nobody here is a known offender. This crime was personal, not professional.

explanation: It was Emeka Obi. The poison was oleander — brewed, not bought — and the Identity Lookup puts an ornamental nursery permit in his name. Phone Records show the Dubai alibi never happened, and Cell Triangulation places his handset on the compound straight through the time of death, long after the "goodnight" he claimed was brief. He poured the dosed glass himself, wiped the decanter (gloved partial print), and let the Chief drink alone. Grace carried the decanter but never touched the glass; the poison was in the glass only. Tunde’s call log and triangulation prove he truly went home. The ₦120m debt died with the Chief.

title: The Silent Ledger
brief: ₦68m vanished from the escrow account of Olumide & Partners over six months, siphoned in amounts just under the audit threshold. The firm’s three signatories all had access. No outsider could have moved the money. One of them is the thief — find the trail.

suspects.name: Kunle Adeyemi
suspects.motive: Deeply in debt to a private lender; his house is mortgaged twice.
suspects.alibi: Was travelling for two of the six transfer dates — abroad, verifiable.
suspects.note: Forthcoming. Volunteered his own bank statements.

suspects.name: Ngozi Eze
suspects.motive: Passed over for partner twice. Knows the audit thresholds better than anyone.
suspects.alibi: In the office on every transfer date. Says she never initiated the transfers.
suspects.note: Precise, guarded. Corrected the detective’s figures twice.

suspects.name: Bashir Lawal
suspects.motive: Quietly funding a second family; his declared income doesn’t cover it.
suspects.alibi: Claims he never uses the online portal — "I sign on paper."
suspects.note: Affable. Insisted the IT logs would clear him.

findings.heading: Pattern
findings.detail: Every transfer is ₦11.4m–₦11.9m — deliberately under the ₦12m amount that triggers a second-signatory review. Whoever did this knew the exact threshold.

findings.heading: Shell account
findings.detail: Opened with a forged utility bill in the name "B. Lawal Holdings." Closed the week the audit began.

findings.heading: Timing
findings.detail: All six transfers initiated between 1:00am and 2:00am — outside office hours, from the firm’s portal.

findings.heading: Credentials
findings.detail: All six 1am logins used BASHIR LAWAL’s username and password — the partner who swears he "never uses the portal."

findings.heading: But the device
findings.detail: The logins came from a workstation on the FINANCE floor, not Bashir’s office. Bashir’s own laptop was offline on four of the six nights.

findings.heading: Password note
findings.detail: Bashir’s password was "Lagos1971" — written on a sticky note under his desk blotter, per the cleaner. Anyone on the finance floor could have lifted it.

witnesses.name: Mr. Okafor
witnesses.statement: "Only one person ever worked past midnight on those nights — the finance lead. The partners were long gone. She said she liked the quiet."

witnesses.name: Cleaner (Tessy)
witnesses.statement: "Oga Bashir keeps his password on a paper under the desk. Everybody on that floor knows. I have told him to stop."

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You were the only person in the office past midnight on every transfer date.

lines.speaker: Ngozi
lines.text: I work late. That is not a crime. Check the logins — they are not mine.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: They used Bashir’s credentials. From a finance-floor workstation. Your floor.

lines.speaker: Ngozi
lines.text: Then talk to Bashir. I cannot help that he leaves his password lying around.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You know the ₦12m threshold to the naira. Who else here does?

lines.speaker: Ngozi
lines.text: ...That is my job. Knowing the rules is not the same as breaking them.

timeline.event: Six transfers, each just under ₦12m, all initiated near 1am.
Bashir’s credentials log in — but from a finance-floor workstation.
Ngozi is the only person in the office past midnight.

results.note: The shell company was never really registered — the name was forged to point at Bashir.

rows.label: All six nights
rows.value: At home — calls placed from his home line after 10pm

results.note: Bashir was home, on his landline, on the nights his credentials were used at the office. He couldn’t have been at the workstation.
Clean records all round — a first-time, inside job.

explanation: It was Ngozi Eze. The Portal Access Log is decisive: the 1am transfers used Bashir’s credentials but came from a FINANCE-floor workstation — her floor — and Bashir’s own laptop was offline on four of those nights, with his call log placing him at home. Bashir’s password sat on a sticky note anyone on the finance floor could lift. Ngozi was the only person in the office past midnight on every transfer date, and she alone knew the ₦12m threshold to the naira. The "B. Lawal Holdings" shell was a forged red herring to frame the senior partner.

title: The Locked Gallery
brief: A ₦90m Ben Enwonwu painting was lifted from the Ido Island gallery during a private viewing. The room was sealed, the alarm never tripped, and the painting’s frame was left hanging — empty — on the wall. Three people had the run of the gallery that night. The canvas hasn’t left the building. Find the thief.

suspects.name: Lara Bankole
suspects.motive: Her contract wasn’t renewed; she leaves at month’s end.
suspects.alibi: Was greeting guests at the entrance, on camera, during the viewing window.

suspects.name: Victor Eze
suspects.motive: Bid ₦70m for the piece last year and was refused.
suspects.alibi: Says he stepped out for "a long phone call" during the viewing.
suspects.note: Smooth. Asked about the gallery’s insurance, not the painting.

suspects.name: Sunday Musa
suspects.motive: Gambling debts; recently asked the curator for an advance.
suspects.alibi: Was in the control room "the whole time," he says, watching the feeds.
suspects.note: Helpful to a fault. Volunteered to walk us through the alarm system.

findings.heading: The cut
findings.detail: The canvas was cut from the frame with a fresh, precise blade — done by someone unhurried, who knew the alarm wouldn’t sound. A panicked outsider doesn’t cut clean.

findings.heading: Alarm
findings.detail: The motion sensor for Room 2 was DISABLED in software for an 18-minute window during the viewing, then re-enabled. No physical tampering.

findings.heading: Glove fibre
findings.detail: Black nitrile fibre on the frame edge — the kind issued to gallery handling staff AND the security technician.

findings.heading: Spilled wine
findings.detail: A guest knocked over red wine near the entrance at 8:10pm. Cleaned up. Unrelated to the theft.

findings.heading: Who disabled it
findings.detail: The Room 2 motion sensor was switched off from the CONTROL-ROOM terminal at 8:31pm — a terminal only the security technician operates during a viewing.

findings.heading: The loop
findings.detail: During the same 18 minutes, the Room 2 camera played an 18-minute loop of empty floor. The looping requires control-room access and the feed password.

findings.heading: Curator’s tablet
findings.detail: Lara’s tablet pinged the gallery Wi-Fi from the ENTRANCE the entire window — she never went near Room 2 or the control room.

witnesses.name: Mrs. Adeyinka
witnesses.statement: "The collector — Victor — was on his phone by the courtyard for a good while. I could see him through the glass the whole time. He never came back inside through Room 2."

witnesses.name: Door steward
witnesses.statement: "Madam Lara was with us at the door, welcoming people, the whole viewing. She did not leave the entrance once."

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: The Room 2 sensor went dark for eighteen minutes. From the control room. Where you were.

lines.speaker: Sunday
lines.text: It must have glitched. The system does that. I re-armed it as soon as I saw.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: And the camera played a loop for exactly those eighteen minutes?

lines.speaker: Sunday
lines.text: A loop? I... maybe a buffer error. I am not a software person.

lines.speaker: Det.
lines.text: You asked the curator for an advance last week. Money trouble?

lines.speaker: Sunday
lines.text: Everybody has money trouble. That does not make me a thief.

timeline.event: A guest spills wine at the entrance; cleaned up.
Victor steps out to the courtyard for a phone call — visible through the glass.
Room 2 motion sensor disabled from the control-room terminal; camera begins an 18-min loop.

results.note: Victor’s handset stayed in the courtyard through the whole theft window. The "long phone call" checks out — he wasn’t in Room 2.

rows.label: Prior employment
rows.value: Dismissed from a casino security role

results.note: He has the exact technical skill to disable a sensor and loop a camera from the control room.
The curator is clean — and the camera/Wi-Fi place her at the entrance throughout.

explanation: It was Sunday Musa, the security technician. The Alarm & Camera Log is conclusive: the Room 2 sensor was disabled and the camera looped from the CONTROL-ROOM terminal he alone operated, during an 18-minute window — and only someone with his alarm/CCTV expertise (per the Identity Lookup) could do both. The clean cut shows an unhurried thief who knew the alarm was off. Lara was at the entrance the whole time (Wi-Fi + the door steward), and Victor’s triangulation keeps him in the courtyard. The wine spill and the handling-glove fibre were noise. Sunday’s gambling debts gave him the motive; the control room gave him the means. The canvas is still hidden in the building.
